SaaS isn't competing with other software anymore… it's competing with labor.

BCG just published its April 2026 Executive Perspective on AI and the future of software and they're missing how this market will actually transform.

Here's what the numbers say:

Roughly $15–20 trillion of global labor is addressable by agentic AI
Only about $3 trillion of that ever becomes software revenue
The rest gets captured directly by the business
IT spend is already shifting away from SaaS toward AI and related enablers

BCG frames this as incumbents versus AI-native startups. But that's the issue... it isn't.

What is an AI agent actually?

Not technically. Operationally. Inside your business, what is it doing?

An agent is not a tool. It is a resource. It takes tasks, makes decisions, executes work. The right way to look at this isn't as software… but as labor. 

Like hiring someone to handle a function in your business... take an agent built to handle reviewing contracts.

What do you do when they join?

You transfer knowledge. What is "auto-renew." How do you identify it in a contract. What qualifies as an approved exception. What is the decision rule when conditions conflict.

Your training your agent on your vocabulary, your logic, your way of assessing contracts. That build up of knowledge is what makes the resource work and work autonomously.

When a vendor manages that agent at scale, they genericize it. Your decision rules map to their data model. Your vocabulary conforms to their design. And where it doesn't fit you create a work around. You absorb the friction and call it process.

Vendors will tell you it's configurable. Within their system, it is. But that misses the point.

The greatest impact from AI agents won't come from automating tasks inside a single tool. It will come from agents working across teams, departments, and systems. 

That is where the real efficiency lives... and exactly where Saas tools breaks down. Every boundary between systems is a translation problem. Every translation problem is a workaround.

You cannot build agent-to-agent precision on top of someone else's architecture.

BCG asks which companies will win the agent market. The better question is who can better manage that semantic layer.

SaaS companies can't solve this with better features or AI embedded in existing workflows. They have to move down a layer... from workflows and UI to the logic that actually runs the business.

For SaaS companies, that means:
🔹 Owning the decision logic, not just the workflow
🔹 Designing systems around your customer's business vocabulary
🔹 Building the capability to encode that knowledge so agents can operate with precision

Saas players that survive will be the ones whose systems understand how a business works well enough for agents to act inside it… without friction, without workarounds, without the in-between killing the efficiency they promised.

You can outsource software. You cannot outsource the logic your business runs on.

The Lone Ranger Problem: Why Healthcare IT Consultants Can't Get Hired

You've trained clinicians in three countries. You've led EHR rollouts for the VA, the DOD, and civilian hospital systems most people have never heard of. You've walked onto a unit on a Monday morning knowing nobody, and by Friday the entire department trusted you with their workflows.

And yet your resume gets filtered out by an ATS before a human ever sees it.
Welcome to the Lone Ranger problem.
If you've spent the last decade as an independent contractor in healthcare IT — hopping from hospital to hospital, implementation to implementation — you know the pattern. You show up. You deliver. The contract ends. You move on. No alumni network. No performance review that follows you. No manager writing your promotion memo. Just a gap in dates and a list of short-term engagements that makes a recruiter's eye twitch.

The problem isn't your experience. The problem is that your career doesn't fit the story corporate hiring was built to read.
HR wants two to three employers, progressive titles, three to five years at each stop. Your resume has fourteen employers in ten years. Title changes from Analyst to Trainer to Adoption Coach to Principal Trainer and back. Three-month stints. A project in Qatar between two in New York.
To anyone who's lived this life, that resume tells a story of range and adaptability. To an ATS scanning for tenure, it tells a story of instability.
You rode in, saved the town, and rode out — and now nobody remembers your name.

Why this is getting worse

The gold rush of major EHR implementations has matured. Projects are smaller, shorter, more competitive. Staffing firms are squeezing rates. Hospitals are building internal teams. And the industry is evolving — AI-enabled documentation, ambient scribes, interoperability mandates. The consultants who know how to train staff on a Cerner upgrade are being asked whether they can also train staff on an AI scribe that writes clinical notes. The skills transfer. The positioning doesn't.
So you end up stuck. Too experienced for entry-level. Too "unstable" for leadership. Too niche for generic roles. Too generalist for emerging AI-specific positions.
What the Lone Ranger actually has
A decade of independent contracting builds a skill set most full-time employees never develop. You can walk into any clinical environment and orient yourself within hours. You can read a room of skeptical clinicians and know within ten minutes whether it's a training problem, a trust problem, or a workflow problem. You can design curriculum from scratch, deliver it across modalities, and adapt on the fly when the go-live plan changes at the last minute. Because it always does.
You've done all of this without a team, without onboarding, without a safety net. Every engagement was a cold start. Every success earned from zero.
That's not instability. Part II (next post)

Hiring for blockchain and Web3 roles is about more than just finding a coder. It’s about building a resilient, compliant, and AI-enabled team that can navigate fast-moving regulation, security demands, and remote-first culture.

Here’s a practical playbook to attract and place top Web3 talent with MyTeamAbroad:

🔸 Start with clarity on your tech stack and the role’s growth path. Blockchain, Solidity, Rust, Node.js, and DevOps aren’t interchangeable. Define the exact mix and how it evolves with product milestones. The clearer the brief, the faster you attract the right specialists.

🔸 Embrace remote-first as a default, not a perk. 70% of 2025 Web3 placements were remote, with hubs like Dubai and Portugal fueling global talent. Offer flexible schedules, time-zone alignment, and asynchronous collaboration to unlock scarce skills without geographic limits.

🔸 Prioritize compliance and security capabilities from day one. The demand for compliance officers rose 40% in Q1 2025 as regulators tighten crypto markets. Integrate AML/KYC, risk assessment, and smart-contract auditing into your core hiring criteria so your project scales safely.

🔸 Look for AI + Web3 fluency. Since late 2024, AI + Web3 hiring jumped 60%, indicating teams need data science, automated contracts, and AI-assisted security tooling. Seek candidates who can blend on-chain engineering with intelligent automation.

🔸 Value depth over breadth for core engineering roles. Strong demand remains for Blockchain/Smart Contract developers, security auditors, and tokenomics specialists. Build a short list of top-tier core skills (Solidity, Rust, Bitcoin-native solutions) and assess depth via real-world problem solving.

🔸 Use a robust, multi-stage vetting pipeline. MyTeamAbroad tests logic and problem solving, then conducts tech exams in each skill area, followed by a CEFR-aligned English evaluation, and a video interview with a psychologist. This holistic approach reveals true capability and cultural fit beyond resumes.

🔸 Offer long-term partnerships, not just staff augmentation. One invoice for the whole team, flexible notice periods, and rapid replacement of underperformers reduce risk and keep momentum. You’re buying a future-ready team, not a temporary fix.

🔸 Prepare for regulatory and visa realities in crypto-friendly hubs. Global expansion means navigating local employment law, visa eligibility, and compliance standards. Plan for onboarding velocity, payroll localization, and local benefits to sustain remote teams long-term.

In a market where TradFi is competing on compensation (up to 30% higher in some cases) and institutional players are snapping up Web3 talent, a deliberate, transparent, and compliant hiring approach becomes your differentiator.

What’s your biggest hurdle in building a Web3 crew right now? Curious how MyTeamAbroad can simplify your search and elevate your team—from contract to long-term partnership.

Hiring an editor is one of the most important investments you'll make in your book—and one of the most personal. The right editor can transform your manuscript. The wrong one can leave you feeling unheard and disheartened.

Here are the questions worth asking before you commit:
* What type of editing do you offer? Developmental editing, copy editing, and proofreading are distinct services. Make sure you're asking for what your manuscript actually needs at this stage.
* What genres do you specialise in? Genre knowledge matters enormously. An editor who loves and understands your genre will bring instinct as well as expertise to the work.
* Do you offer a sample edit? A short sample edit, even a page or two, tells you more about an editor's style and approach than any amount of testimonials.
* What does your contract cover? A professional contract should address confidentiality, timelines, fees, revision rounds—and their position on AI tools.
* What can I expect to receive? Clarify exactly what's included—tracked changes, an editorial report, a follow-up call. Know what you're getting before you sign.

The right editor is out there. Take your time, ask the right questions, and trust your instincts. Full blog post in the comments—including what to expect on timelines, costs, and formats.

Defence budgets have never been bigger. The talent pool has never been smaller. 

A new report from Oliver Wyman and the Milken Institute puts the number plainly: Europe needs 250,000 additional engineers and skilled technicians within five years just to meet current defence demand. That's more than 25% on top of the entire existing workforce. And that's before you account for the specialist end of the market.

The UK is at the centre of this.

The Strategic Defence Review set a 10 year roadmap towards warfighting readiness. A new £400m annual Defence Innovation unit is fast-tracking AI, simulation, and deep tech to frontline use. The MOD committed £6.6bn to defence R&D with immersive and emerging technology explicitly in scope. The contracts are flowing, and increasingly to SMEs and non-traditional suppliers. 

Simulation and XR have crossed the line from emerging tech to established capability.

The Royal Navy just completed a £27m simulator refit at HMS Collingwood using XR headsets. Bohemia Interactive Simulations holds a live MOD contract for virtual training environments across all branches. Defence simulation is an established, funded, growing sector in the UK. The studios and solution providers building in this space are hiring now. 

The talent serving this market is exceptionally niche.

Simulation developers. Graphics engineers with defence exposure. ML specialists who understand synthetic environments. XR generalists who've worked anywhere close to a cleared programme. This isn't a pool you casually dip into on LinkedIn. Most of these people aren't looking. Many can't publicly state what they've worked on. 

Internal referrals and job ads won't scale.

The defence sector has always run on internal networks. That works...until it doesn't. And right now with every organisation hiring simultaneously, it doesn't. 

The companies winning the talent game right now have someone in their corner.

Not for every hire. For the hard ones. The roles that sit open for months because the profile doesn't exist on any job board. Having a specialist who can pitch your company, your studio, your programme, your mission into a network that the primes don't have access to. That's the edge.

We work in this space every day. If 2026 is a hiring year for you, it's worth a conversation even before the role is open.

San Francisco. Washington. Brussels.

Three cities. Three consensuses. Three completely different theories of how the AI future actually arrives.

And every leader is already operating from one of them — most without realizing it.

1. The San Francisco Consensus
Everything changes in the next 5 years
Technology sets the timeline. Markets adapt. Disruption is the point.
UBI becomes necessary, not optional
Radical life extension within reach
Careers as we know them are over
Robots in most households

2. The Washington Consensus
The state sets the timeline. AI is strategic capability — to be governed, deployed, and contested.
Industrial policy decides the winners
Chips, energy and alliances are the real battlefield
Workforce reskilling becomes national security
Speed matters, but only if you control it
AI is Geopolitics

3. The Brussels Consensus
AI will impact society.... but it will be gradual 
Institutions set the timeline. Rights and legitimacy come first.
Regulation precedes deployment
The social contract is non-negotiable
Trust is the long-term moat
Better late and trusted than fast and broken

I am currently advising companies operating from all three.
And here is what I see from inside the room.
You are already operating from one of these consensuses.
Your hiring plan reveals it.
Your automation roadmap reveals it.
Your board deck reveals it.
You don't get to opt out.
If you are running a San Francisco strategy inside a Brussels regulatory environment, you will hit a wall.
If you are running a Brussels strategy in a San Francisco market, you will be outbuilt.
If you are running a Washington strategy, you have to win on speed and legitimacy at the same time — and most teams are built for one or the other.
Your choice of consensus is the most important strategic decision your organization will make this decade.

And most leadership teams have never named theirs out loud.
So name it.
A = San Francisco
B = Washington
C = Brussels

Comment your letter, and provide some context to how you see the development. I will share what I am seeing across leadership teams in each camp.

Or drop us a message for a confidential chat.

Your firm knows about AI. Nobody has moved.

There's a meeting where someone mentions it. Everyone nods. Nothing gets decided.

That's not a strategy. That's drift with good posture.

The firms that missed the last major move in legal didn't ignore it. They discussed it until it was too late to lead it.

The signal isn't in the technology. It's in the hiring decisions firms are making, or not making, right now.

Legal AI funding crossed $1 billion in 2024. These startups aren't building tools to assist lawyers. They're building infrastructure to replace entire workflow layers. Contract review. Due diligence. Legal research. The commodity work junior associates have always done.

The Dot.Com era felt like this. Uncertain. Overhyped. Then, suddenly, structural.

The firms that survived it weren't the ones who understood the internet first. They were the ones who changed what they were building before the market forced them to.

Here's what that looks like in practical terms right now:

1. Audit what your junior fee earners actually do. If 60% of their hours are tasks AI can already handle, your leverage model has a problem worth naming.

2. Stop hiring for volume. Start hiring for judgement. The lawyers who will matter in 5 years are the ones who know what to do with an AI output, not the ones producing it.

3. Build AI literacy into your next senior hire brief. Not as a nice-to-have. As a requirement. Someone who can't navigate these tools won't be able to supervise the people who can.

4. Find one workflow to test it on. Not a committee. Not a 14-person pilot. One workflow. One team. Real feedback in 90 days.

5. Decide what your firm is actually for. If the answer is still "we do the work," you're describing a process. The firms that lead this will answer differently: we make the judgement. AI does the work.

This isn't about becoming a tech company.

It's about not being the firm that held 12 meetings about the internet and still has a website nobody uses.

The window isn't closed. But it's not wide open either.

AI is already reshaping legal. That part isn't a prediction.

The only question is whether your firm is being shaped by it, or is one of the ones doing the shaping.

Most European SMBs asking about private AI get stuck on the same question before they even start.
Do we buy GPUs, rent them, or pay someone to manage the whole thing?

The answer depends on one number: how many GPU-hours per month your workload actually needs.
Buying an 8-GPU server costs between €200,000 and €450,000 upfront — before networking, cooling, power, and the specialist staff to run it. At 1,000 GPU-hours monthly, cloud rental costs €25,000 a year. Owned infrastructure in year one can exceed €1,000,000.

For most SMBs, the math is clear. Ownership makes economic sense only at very high and sustained GPU utilization, a threshold most SMBs never reach.
But renting from a public cloud provider introduces a different problem.

When your AI model processes client contracts, patient records, or financial data on AWS or Azure infrastructure, that data leaves your environment. It may sit on servers subject to US jurisdiction. For European companies in regulated sectors, that is not a technical question. It is a compliance question.

The third option, managed private AI on EU infrastructure, addresses both constraints. You pay per use, without capital expenditure. Your data stays within EU jurisdiction. Your models run on dedicated resources, not shared infrastructure.

For organizations without an existing AI engineering team, building that capability internally costs more than most expect - in time, in hiring, and in operational overhead that has nothing to do with your actual use case.

At Armored Cloud, private AI infrastructure runs on EU-based dedicated resources. We handle the infrastructure, the compliance, and the operational overhead.
Not owned, not shared - managed, in Europe.

🇨🇦 From a Recruiter’s Perspective | Rooting for you | Why the Job Market Feels So Off Right Now 😅

You’re getting interviews…
But not offers.

You’re making it to final rounds…
But not getting picked.

You’re thinking… is it me?

It’s not just you.

The March job market did something weird.

It didn’t crash.
Didn’t bounce either.

It just… hovered.

Like refreshing your inbox thinking this is the one…
and it’s a sale email 😅

Here’s what actually shifted 👇

Full-time roles quietly took the hit.

Yes, jobs were added overall…

But underneath?

More part-time
More contract
More “let’s wait and see”

So full-time roles?

Packed. Competitive. A little chaotic.

(Think: hundreds of applicants, multiple rounds… and a decision that takes weeks 😅)

Across Canada 🇨🇦

Ontario – Busy, but slow decisions
Quebec – Steady, bilingual advantage
Alberta – Some movement, still cautious
BC – Cooling, more contract roles
Prairies – Stable, consistent
Atlantic – Smaller, tighter, network-driven

And here’s the part people feel but don’t always see…

Retail is still hiring.
But with more candidates per role.

Hospitality?

Hiring… but uneven.

(“We’re hiring!” and “we’re cutting hours” happening at the same time 😅)

So what’s causing all this?

Budgets under a microscope
Economic uncertainty lingering
AI + restructuring shifting roles

So companies are doing what?

Waiting.

Waiting for clarity
Waiting for budgets
Waiting for someone else to go first

(Everyone’s looking around like… not it 😅)

And this creates…

The “almost” candidate.

You’re getting interviews
You’re getting close
You’re thinking this is it

And then…

Another round
A delay
A “we’re aligning internally”

(Which usually means… still deciding 😅)

Quick tips if you’re in it right now:

Be specific on why you want that company
Tell your story clearly (don’t make them connect the dots)
Use your recruiter (we’re the cheat sheet)
Follow up like a human

And don’t take the “almost” personally.

This market is slower. Pickier. A little indecisive.

You’re closer than it feels.

Rooting for you always 🇨🇦

Be honest…

Are you getting interviews but no offers right now?

you're not looking for someone who knows LLMs.

And fixed it.

Most AI product leaders are exceptional at building the roadmap.
Very few can walk it into a regulated enterprise — and come out with a signed contract.
These are two completely different skills.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
After 21 years across IBM, ADP, and Vitech, here is what separates them:
The first skill is product craft. The second is enterprise trust architecture.
→ Can you explain your AI's decision to an audit committee? → Can you get a Fortune 500 CISO to approve your agent's access controls? → Can you compress a compliance review from 6 months to 6 weeks — without cutting corners?
Most AI leaders are exceptional at the first.
Almost none have lived the second.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
I have.
$180M in Agentic AI pipeline at IBM. 77,000 enterprise clients at ADP. 0 to 22 product org built from scratch at Vitech. Zero audit findings across the full pilot period.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The companies winning enterprise AI in 2026 aren't the ones with the most innovative models.
They're the ones with product leaders who know how to make AI trustworthy — to the people who write the cheques.
That combination is rare.
And I'm available.

The "Social Contract" is officially being rewritten this month. Between the Fair Work Agency (FWA) launch (who have enforcement powers) and the shift to Day One Rights (currently family friendly rights and SSP), the administrative burden on HR has never been higher—but neither has the opportunity to lead.

If your April strategy is just about updating the employee handbook, you’re only solving half the problem. Here is how I’m viewing the current landscape:

1. From "Probation" to "Performance"
With the move toward shorter unfair dismissal windows and instant rights, the "wait and see" approach to hiring is dead. We have to get better at identifying skills + achievements during the interview stage, not six months into the job.

2. The Agentic AI Shift
We’ve moved past simple Generative AI. 2026 is the year of Agentic AI—systems that don’t just write emails but actually audit our compliance and flag payroll discrepancies in real-time. If your HR tech isn't doing the heavy lifting, your team will burn out on the paperwork.

We are moving from an era of "HR as a Support Function" to "HR as a Risk & Performance Architect."

#HR #FutureOfWork #PeopleStrategy #Leadership #HRTech #EmploymentLaw2026

The real estate industry in 2026 is no longer just about "buying and selling houses." It has evolved into a high-tech, multi-trillion dollar global engine driven by rapid urbanization, institutional investment, and the rise of Global Capability Centers (GCCs).

🏗️ The Growth Drivers of 2026
The industry is currently experiencing a structural shift. While traditional residential sales remain strong, new sectors are fueling the most aggressive growth: 

Commercial Evolution: Global corporations are treating "office space" as an innovation hub. In major markets like India, GCCs now account for nearly 48% of all office leasing, making Grade A commercial assets a top-tier investment.
The "Luxury" Boom: There is a massive surge in demand for premium, larger homes. In cities like Delhi-NCR and Mumbai, luxury residential prices have seen an annual appreciation of roughly 15%. 

Demographic Shift: Approximately 74% of homebuyers are now under the age of 35. This "Millennial and Gen Z" wave is forcing developers to focus on smart homes, co-living spaces, and sustainable "green" buildings. 

Alternative Assets: Beyond apartments and offices, capital is flowing into Data Centers, Warehousing, and Healthcare real estate, which are growing by 10% annually.

💼 The "Power Careers" in Real Estate
The career landscape has shifted from basic sales to high-finance and tech-driven roles. Here are the most powerful career paths today:

1. Real Estate Private Equity (REPE) Associate
These professionals manage large pools of institutional money to buy entire portfolios of property. 

Potential: Extremely high (Avg. $130k – $300k+ with "carry" or profit-sharing).
Role: Financial modeling, deal sourcing, and asset management.

2. Commercial Real Estate (CRE) Broker
Unlike residential agents, CRE brokers deal with skyscrapers, malls, and industrial parks.
Potential: $200k – $500k+ (Uncapped commissions).
Role: Negotiating multi-million dollar leases and sales for corporations.

3. Real Estate Wholesaler & Investor
For those with an entrepreneurial spirit, wholesaling is the "fast track" because it requires little to no startup capital.
Potential: $50k – $150k+ (Can scale to $1M+).
Role: Finding distressed properties, securing them under contract, and "flipping the paperwork" to cash investors for a fee.

4. PropTech & Data Analytics
As the industry becomes data-driven, companies are hiring experts to predict market trends using AI.

🎨 Klantendienst Medewerker – Verf & Coatings (Vilvoorde)- VAST CONTRACT
 
Voor een toonaangevende speler actief binnen de DIY-, verf- en coatingssector, zijn we op zoek naar een Klantendienst Medewerker voor de regio Vilvoorde.
Hou je ervan klanten verder te helpen met praktisch advies en duidelijke antwoorden? Dan is dit misschien jouw volgende uitdaging.
 
🖌️ Jouw functie
 
Als Klantendienst Medewerker ben jij het eerste aanspreekpunt voor particuliere klanten en doe‑het‑zelvers. Je helpt hen professioneel en vriendelijk verder met vragen over producten, toepassingen en keuzes binnen verf en coatings.
 
🧰Wat ga je doen?
 
°Klanten ondersteunen via telefoon, e-mail en sociale media
°Advies geven over productgebruik, toepassingen, kleurkeuze en ondergronden
°Vragen en klachten correct registreren en opvolgen in het CRM-systeem
°Samenwerken met collega’s en productexperten om tot de juiste oplossing te komen
°Productinformatie actueel houden op digitale kanalen
°Actief meedenken over digitale optimalisaties (chatflows, online support, social media)
°Meewerken aan de uitrol van digitale tools (bv. Power BI, AI-oplossingen)
°Terugkerende vragen en trends signaleren en intern delen
°Werken binnen een hecht Benelux-team (BE & NL)
 
👤 Jouw profiel
 
°Je voelt je thuis in een klantgerichte rol en hebt interesse in verf, coatings en DIY‑toepassingen.
°Bachelor denkniveau of gelijkwaardig door ervaring
°Zeer goede kennis van Nederlands én Frans (± 50/50)
°Affiniteit met verf, coatings, schilderwerken of DIY is een mooie plus
OF:
°een bachelor met relevante ervaring en sterke digitale vaardigheden
(bv. Power BI, AI, data-analyse of automatisatie)
°Kennis van SAP, MS Office en/of Power BI is een troef
°Communicatief, klantgericht en stressbestendig
°Je blijft professioneel, ook bij kritische klanten
°Je werkt graag in een rustige, gestructureerde teamomgeving
 
🎁 Wat mag je verwachten?
 
°Voltijds contract (40u/week) met vaste uren van 9u tot 17u
°4/5de mogelijk voor sterke kandidaten
°2 dagen thuiswerk per week na opleiding
°18 ADV-dagen, maaltijdcheques & uitgebreid verzekeringspakket
°Aantrekkelijke verloning binnen de chemie sector volgens ervaring
°Stabiele werkgever met warme werksfeer en fijne collega’s
°Regelmatige bedrijfsevents
°Locatie op 10 minuten wandelen van station Vilvoorde
°Interne opleiding door productexperten – je groeit on the job

Recruiting is one of the most important jobs in the economy.

Every great company gets built by the people its founders chose to hire. 

Every meaningful career has a recruiter behind it somewhere. The one who picked up the phone, made the case, built the relationship over years.

The tools the industry has been given don't act like that's true.

For 15 years, recruiting CRMs have turned a relationship craft into a data-entry job. Source. Tag. Filter. Update the pipeline. Write the email. Update the pipeline again. £150/user/month for software built for 500-person staffing groups, sold to 5-person UK agencies.

We started @Shorltists because one solo recruiter inside Bifrost Studios - our Copenhagen venture studio - was paying enterprise prices for a CRM built for an enterprise she wasn't part of. We dug in and realised the same problem hits 95% of UK recruitment.

So we partnered with the recruiters we trusted, and built the tool they wished existed.

Shortlists is the recruiting CRM, ATS, and BD Radar built for 2-to-10 person UK agencies.

→ One tool, not five. Replaces your CRM, ATS, BD tracker, AI notetaker, and client portal. 
→ £62/user/month. No annual contract. No hidden add-ons. 
→ BD Radar reads every recruiting board, funding round, and leadership change overnight - and surfaces fee-earning roles before they're posted publicly. 
→ Free CTO-led migration from Bullhorn / Vincere / Loxo / Atlas - overnight, before your next working day starts.

The job-to-be-done: get to the hiring manager before the job hits LinkedIn.
When AI takes the admin layer, the craft is what's left. The next generation of recruiting CRMs will be the ones that respect that.

If you're running a 2-to-10 person UK agency, DM me.

7-Eleven just automated 95 percent of its hiring process through Workday's AI agents. Not assisted. Not accelerated. Automated.

Workday's Innovation Summit last week was full of numbers like this. 75 percent of their 11,500 customers are using Workday AI in core processes. NetApp cut 24,000 contracts down to 600 in days. These are not pilots. These are production results.

What the coverage keeps glossing over: the customers getting these results are not the ones who bought the best AI feature. They're the ones who did the organisational work first. Every customer panel at the Summit circled back to the same three blockers — data quality, process clarity, and culture. The AI didn't solve those. It rewarded the companies that already had.

That's the uncomfortable implication for mid-market leaders watching from the sidelines. Workday's agentic architecture is moving fast. But if your HR or finance processes are messy, your data is inconsistent, and your teams haven't agreed on what good looks like — an agent will automate the mess, not fix it. You won't get a dramatic reduction in payroll query handling time. You'll get faster errors.

The companies getting results right now didn't find a better tool. They changed something structurally before they switched anything on.

What would your processes look like if an agent had to execute them tomorrow, without a human to paper over the gaps?

Follow Metis Point — technology strategy without the big-firm overhead.
Source: diginomica

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗥𝗥 𝗖𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆

A 105% NRR can be a green flag. Or it can be a cover story.

Same average. Different company.

Most PE-backed software businesses are reporting the first and living in the second.

What's driving it isn't cyclical.

The COVID hiring surge has fully unwound. Fewer people means fewer seats. Roles that don't come back don't renew.

AI is compounding it. Quietly. Teams that needed twelve analysts now need eight. The work still gets done. The licenses don't get renewed.

And procurement reset the default. Not just cuts, the buying behaviour changed. Audit first. Renew only what's demonstrably used. That doesn't reverse when budgets recover.

The headcount model shifted too. Where hiring has come back, teams are leaner and more automated. The seat count no longer follows headcount the way it used to.

All of this gets compressed into one NRR number that looks stable right up until a buyer breaks it apart.

A healthy business expands across the account base. A concentrated one looks identical from the outside. Until you separate the top accounts from everything else. One is growing. The other is quietly contracting.

You don't have a retention story. You have concentration masking decline.

The top expands on usage. The tail contracts on seats.

If you don't know the split, a buyer will.

#PrivateEquity #ValueCreation #ExitReadiness

Here's exactly where AI adds genuine value in a business workflow — and where it doesn't.

Where AI genuinely helps:
→ Classifying unstructured inputs (WhatsApp messages, emails, form responses) into structured categories 
→ Drafting first versions of repetitive written outputs (summaries, proposals, responses) 
→ Extracting specific data from messy documents (invoices, contracts, intake forms) 
→ Routing decisions that require reading intent, not just matching keywords → Summarising long threads or histories before a human needs to act

Where AI adds risk, not value:
→ Final decisions on anything with real consequences (payments, contracts, hiring) 
→ Customer-facing communications that need deep relationship context 
→ Processes that are still being defined — AI bakes in the wrong version 
→ Anything where the error cost is higher than the efficiency gain 
→ Replacing human judgment on complex, novel problems

The pattern:
AI is great at the first pass — interpreting, extracting, drafting, routing. Humans are better at the final judgment — deciding, relating, contextualising.

Build your AI layer to handle the first pass. 
Build your human layer to handle everything AI shouldn't touch.

That's the architecture that actually works.

We are #hiring for a Digital Marketing Specialist (Contract)

About The Solid Corp
The Solid Corp is a creative digital agency and MarTech consultancy based in Bangalore. We specialize in building technical infrastructure and AI-driven growth strategies to help brands navigate the evolving search and discovery landscape. We are currently seeking a disciplined, data-driven Digital Marketing Specialist to join our team on a contract basis.

Nature of Engagement
This is a freelance/contract position. The engagement will be structured around specific project deliverables or a recurring monthly retainer, depending on client requirements.


Key Responsibilities
• Performance Marketing: End-to-end management of Google Ads campaigns, including Search, Display, and Performance Max, with a strict focus on conversion metrics and ROI.
• SEO and Organic Growth: Execution of technical and on-page SEO strategies. This includes adapting to modern search trends such as Answer Engine Optimization (AEO).
• Analytics and Attribution: Managing GA4 and Google Tag Manager setups to ensure accurate tracking, data integrity, and transparent reporting.
• Conversion Optimization: Collaborating with our development and design teams to optimize landing pages for maximum campaign efficiency.

Required Qualifications
• 1–2 years of professional experience in digital marketing, specifically within performance and organic growth channels.
• Demonstrated ability to manage Google Ads budgets and drive measurable growth.
• Strong understanding of technical SEO fundamentals and current search engine algorithms.
• Familiarity with CMS platforms such as Webflow or Framer is a significant advantage.
• A data-centric mindset with the ability to translate complex metrics into actionable strategic insights.


Application Process
Interested candidates should submit a resume and a brief summary of a specific campaign or growth project they have successfully managed.
Please send your application to: harish@thesolidcorp.com

Hello Everyone,
We are Hiring for AI QA Analyst / QA Prompt Engineer - Remote

Job Title: AI QA Analyst / QA Prompt Engineer
Location: Remote – India (Supporting US Time Zone)
Job Type: Long Term Contract 
Shift: Night Shift (US Time Zone)
Must have 6+years of experience 


We are seeking an entry-to-mid level QA professional to join the Quality Center of Excellence team in an AI-focused test design role. This role will support multiple project teams across the organization by using AI tools to accelerate and improve the creation of high-quality test cases, test scenarios, and coverage strategies. In addition to AI-led test design support, this person will help maintain quality assets in qTest and contribute to the long-term vision of integrating approved test cases into Tosca-based automation workflows.


Key Responsibilities

• Support project teams with AI-assisted test design based on user stories, requirements, functional specifications, technical specifications, future-state process documents, scenarios, and existing test assets.
• Analyze project documentation and prior sprint artifacts to create effective prompts that produce high-quality, high-coverage test cases with minimal back-and-forth.
• Act as an AI SME for assigned projects, owning AI-based test case generation and prompt refinement for those teams.
• Design and refine prompts for tools such as ChatGPT, Copilot, and other LLM-based assistants to generate relevant and executable test cases.

Required Qualifications and Core Skills

• 6+ years of experience in software testing, QA, or quality engineering.
• Basic working knowledge of software testing principles, test design techniques, test execution, defect tracking, test management tools, and quality assurance processes.
• Experience working in Agile/Scrum environments with sprints, user stories, and backlog-driven delivery.
• Working knowledge of prompt engineering for QA and test design, including the ability to create structured prompts that improve output quality, reduce iteration, and increase test coverage.
• Ability to analyze user stories and historical test assets from prior sprints in order to design better prompts and generate more accurate, reusable test cases.
• Familiarity with AI-assisted documentation and content generation using tools such as ChatGPT, Copilot, or similar generative AI solutions in a testing or business context.
• Hands-on comfort with test management tools and asset maintenance activities; exposure to qTest is strongly preferred.
• Exposure to or willingness to learn Tosca automation, Tricentis products, and automation-readiness concepts for manual test cases.
• Tricentis, Tosca, qTest, or other testing tool certifications are preferred where available.

The Hidden Revenue Leakage in Contract Lifecycle Management 

Many organisations buy CLM platforms expecting instant transformation, but software cannot fix broken ownership, conflicting incentives, or unmanaged commercial workflows.

Missed renewals destroy revenue 🚩
Pricing inconsistency erodes margin 🚩
Slow approvals delay cash 🚩
Weak controls increase risk 🚩
Manual reporting slows decisions 🚩

No automation layer. No risk detection logic. No alerting model. No control thresholds by design.  

If your systems don't share the same truth, the tool will only amplify confusion and support poor decisions.

Many firms chase 3–15% AI uplift in selected functions while ignoring far larger value leakage embedded in contracts, pricing, renewals, and obligations.

AI can create targeted gains, but many organisations overlook structural leakage in contract lifecycle management that can materially exceed any isolated AI benefits.

Contracts are not documents. 
They are the operating system of enterprise value. 

The biggest CLM opportunity is rarely the tool itself. It is redesigning the operating model architecture around revenue, control, risk, and accountability, while ensuring enterprise systems operate as one coherent platform rather than siloed entities with different realities.

If you see similarities in your current operating model, you already know this will not be solved by another tool or by adding more specialist headcount. 

Even worse is hiring people who sell AI hype or manual process management while leaving the underlying operating model and target state unchanged. This is not an enterprise built for scale.

This problem will be resolved through clear strategy, structural redesign, and disciplined execution.

📊 The Reality of Today’s Job Market

The job market right now is competitive, selective, and fast‑changing.
Companies are hiring — but they are hiring carefully.
Roles are fewer, expectations are higher, and skills matter more than titles.

Here’s what I’m seeing across industries:
✅ Skills over degrees – Practical knowledge is winning
✅ Experience > keywords on resumes
✅ Contract & project roles are increasing
✅ AI, data, automation, and operations skills are in high demand
✅ Soft skills (communication, problem‑solving, ownership) matter more than ever

For job seekers, this means:

1.Keep upskilling
2.Customize your resume and LinkedIn profile
3.Focus on impact, not just responsibilities
4.Stay consistent and patient

✅ The market is tough — but it’s not closed ✅

Those who adapt, learn, and stay visible will win.

 Collaborate with business and technology stakeholders to define end-to-end
data solutions.
2. Data Modelling & Implementation
 Design and maintain conceptual, logical, and physical data models for
analytics and reporting.
 Build and manage ETL/ELT pipelines using GCP services like Dataflow,
Dataproc, and BigQuery.
 Ensure data governance, lineage, and quality controls are integrated into
solutions.
3. Performance Tuning & Optimization
 Optimize data pipelines, queries, and storage layers for scalability and
performance.
 Fine-tune BigQuery cost performance, partitioning, and clustering
strategies.
 Implement monitoring and alerting for proactive performance management.
4. Leadership, Mentoring
 Lead, guide, and mentor a team of data engineers, enabling technical upskills
and growth.
 Conduct code reviews, design validations, and architecture discussions.
 Champion best practices for data engineering, governance, and cloud-native
development.
Skills & Qualifications
Required Skills
 4+ years of hands-on experience with GCP data engineering services,
including:
o BigQuery, DataFlow, Cloud Run, Cloud Composer, Data Stream,
Data Fusion, Cloud SQL, Vertex AI, GBQ Workflow, DataPrep, Data
Fusion, DataForm

 Strong expertise in SQL and Python / PySpark for data transformations.

Classification | INTERNAL

 Hands-on experience in ETL/ELT pipeline development and data
modelling.
 Familiarity with CI/CD for data pipelines using tools like Cloud Build, Git,
Jenkins.
Preferred Skills
 Experience with real-time streaming frameworks (e.g., Pub/Sub, Kafka,
Spark Streaming).
 Knowledge of data lakehouse architectures and open-source tools like
Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake, or Hudi.
 Understanding of data security, IAM roles, DLP policies, and compliance
frameworks in GCP.
 Hands-on experience working in Agile/Scrum environments.
#Dataengineer #GCP

✈️We Are Hiring Freelancers | Multiple Roles
At Nextloop Technologies, we are expanding our project teams and looking for talented freelancers to join us on exciting engagements with global clients. If you’re passionate about IT consulting and want to work on high-impact projects, explore our current openings below:

Nextloop Technologies is a leading provider of digital transformation services, specializing in Staff augmentation, customs of software developers and AI and Blockchain solutions, enterprise software development, SAP consulting, cloud solutions, data analytics. We partner with global enterprises to deliver innovative and scalable solutions.

✅Requirements:
We are seeking a highly skilled SAP Payroll Consultant to join our project team at Nextloop Technologies. The freelancer will be responsible for delivering high- quality solutions aligned with client need.

✔️Skill- Set Required:
· Strong expertise in SAP HCM Payroll (US Payroll Processing) 
· Hands-on experience with end-to-end payroll execution, including validations, reconciliation, and adjustments 
· Familiarity with US payroll taxes and compliance (FIT, FICA, FUTA, SUTA, W-2, 941 filings) 
· Additional knowledge of payroll testing, SAP integrations, and multi-state payroll processing 
 
📌Location: Remote
 
⌛Tenure for project:
Project Duration – 12 months Contract
Extensions possible based on performance and project needs.
 
📑Experience:
8–9 years of relevant professional experience in SAP HCM Payroll (US Payroll) with a proven track record of successful payroll implementations and support engagements.
 
Why Join Us?
Collaborate on prestigious projects with global enterprises.
Flexible freelance opportunities across diverse technologies.
Exposure to cutting-edge IT solutions and digital transformation.
Competitive compensation and professional growth.

📩How to Apply :
Send your updated resume to hr@nextlooptechnologies.com with the subject line Application – SAP Payroll Consultant | Nextloop Technologies.
